title: Retrieval Isn't One-Size-Fits-All
concept: Framing the dense vs sparse choice as query-dependent rather than a single global decision.

exercises.prompt: As covered in the previous lesson, which scenario plays directly to the strengths of sparse retrieval?

options.text: Matching exact product codes and rare identifiers
Understanding the emotional tone of a paragraph

exercises.explain: Sparse retrieval (like BM25) excels at exact matches, such as product serial numbers, error codes, and unique identifiers, where semantic meaning is less important than character-for-character precision.

cards.title: One Size Fits None
cards.body: We often try to find the ultimate retrieval algorithm that wins every benchmark. In reality, the best system doesn't choose one method; it dynamically adapts to what the user is looking for.

cards.title: Query-Dependent Routing
cards.body: Instead of forcing one retrieval system to handle every search, query-dependent routing analyzes the incoming question first. It routes precise keyword searches to sparse models and conceptual questions to dense models.

cards.title: The Tool Belt Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a toolbox with a surgical scalpel (sparse retrieval) and a kitchen sponge (dense retrieval). You don't glue them together for every job; you choose the tool based on what you need to pick up: a precise speck of dust or a wide puddle of water.

cards.title: Smart Routing in Action
cards.body: A developer builds a support chatbot. When a user inputs 'Error code 404', the system routes it to a sparse BM25 index. When a user asks 'Why is my app loading slowly?', it routes the query to a dense vector database.

cards.title: The 'Either-Or' Trap
cards.body: Many developers believe they must choose either dense or sparse retrieval for their entire codebase. In truth, the most robust production systems combine them, using routing or hybridization to play to each one's strengths.

cards.title: Visualizing the Decision
cards.body: Compare how a router handles two completely different user intents:

left.text: Query: 'Error 502 Bad Gateway'. Matches exact error log files.

right.text: Query: 'What causes connection drops?' Matches general network troubleshooting guides.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following user queries is best routed to a sparse retrieval system instead of a dense semantic system?

options.text: How do I fix a leaky pipe?
Tips for writing clean Python code.

exercises.explain: Exact identifiers like serial numbers, codes, and product SKUs lack semantic meaning, making them perfect candidates for sparse keyword matching rather than dense vector searches.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once you build a dense retrieval vector database, you must delete your sparse keyword indexes because they cannot coexist.
exercises.explain: A system can run both retrieval methods and merge their results, or use a router to choose the best one per query. They are not mutually exclusive.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept about dynamically directing user queries:
exercises.sentence: Using dynamic routing allows an application to select the best search method for each query.
exercises.explain: Routing allows the system to send keyword-heavy queries to a sparse index and conceptual queries to a dense index, optimizing accuracy.

exercises.prompt: Identify the explanation that was written by an AI trying to sound overly complex:

options.text: Sparse retrieval excels when the user searches for specific part numbers or code snippets.
Dense retrieval is ideal for natural language questions where the exact words don't match the database.
We route low-entropy lexicographical tokens to sparse pipelines and high-dimensional semantic spaces to dense pipelines.

exercises.explain: Option C is generated by an AI because it unnecessarily uses complex jargon ('low-entropy lexicographical tokens') to describe simple keyword terms, while the others are standard explanations.

exercises.prompt: Your application handles both natural questions ('how do I reset my password') and precise error codes ('Error 0x80070005'). What is the most efficient, modern architecture pattern to handle both query types?

options.text: Convert all sparse documents into dense vectors to force a single retrieval pipeline.
Use dense retrieval for all queries because semantic understanding is always superior.
Analyze the query's characteristics and route it to the retrieval method that fits best.
Rely entirely on BM25 for the first stage and discard semantic embeddings.

exercises.explain: Rather than choosing a single retrieval method for your entire application, you can analyze incoming queries dynamically. Technical searches with exact error codes or serial numbers go to sparse retrieval, while vague, conceptual user queries go to dense retrieval.
